title: Volume of Intersecting Cylinders

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-03-calculus-linear-algebra.md

setup.narrative: Two infinite cylinders, each of radius 1, intersect at right angles with their axes meeting at the origin. The first cylinder's axis lies along the x-axis; the second along the y-axis.
rules: Compute the volume of their intersection.
setup.question: What is the volume of the intersection?

learningObjectives: Apply the slicing formula V = integral of A(z) dz to a 3D solid.
Determine the cross-sectional shape A(z) at height z.
Use symmetry about z = 0 to simplify the integral bounds.

stages.label: Choose your technique
stages.prompt: How do you compute the volume of a 3D solid?

options.text: V = integral of A(z) dz, where A(z) is the cross-sectional area at height z.

stages.explanation: The slicing formula. Cut the solid into thin horizontal slabs at each height z; each slab has volume A(z) dz; integrate.

stages.label: Find the cross-section at height z
stages.prompt: Cut the intersection with the horizontal plane at height z (where -1 <= z <= 1). The cross-section is the set of (x, y) satisfying both cylinder constraints. What shape is it?

options.text: A square with side 2 * sqrt(1 - z^2).
A diamond with diagonals sqrt(1 - z^2).

stages.explanation: Cylinder 1 constraint at height z: y^2 + z^2 <= 1, so |y| <= sqrt(1 - z^2). Cylinder 2 constraint: |x| <= sqrt(1 - z^2). Both constraints together define a square -- |x| <= sqrt(1-z^2) AND |y| <= sqrt(1-z^2). Side length: 2 * sqrt(1 - z^2). Area: [2 * sqrt(1 - z^2)]^2 = 4(1 - z^2).

stages.label: Compute A(z)
stages.prompt: What is A(z), the cross-sectional area at height z?
stages.explanation: Square with side 2 sqrt(1 - z^2) has area [2 sqrt(1 - z^2)]^2 = 4(1 - z^2).

stages.label: Integrate using symmetry
stages.prompt: V = integral from -1 to 1 of A(z) dz = integral from -1 to 1 of 4(1-z^2) dz. Using symmetry about z = 0 (so V = 2 * integral from 0 to 1), compute V.

stages.label: Commit final answer
stages.prompt: What is the volume of the intersection?
stages.explanation: 16/3, exactly. (Sanity check via the inscribed-sphere method: a unit sphere has volume (4/3)*pi and lives inside the intersection. At each z, sphere cross-section / square cross-section = pi(1-z^2) / 4(1-z^2) = pi/4 -- a constant ratio. So V_intersection = (4/pi) * V_sphere = (4/pi) * (4*pi/3) = 16/3. ✓)

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: What is the volume if THREE mutually perpendicular cylinders all of radius 1 intersect? (Steinmetz solid -- requires more thought.)
Why does the inscribed-sphere ratio (pi/4) persist at every z? What makes it constant?
If the cylinders had different radii r_1 and r_2, what's the volume?
